skill.name: gen-env
skill.description: Creates and reviews gen-env workflows for isolated local development instances with separate ports, Docker resources, hosts, and generated env files.
skill.summary: Generate isolated local development environments for parallel project instances.
skill.author: 0xBigBoss

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, filesystem, network, and environment-variable patterns. Manual review indicates these are primarily documentation and reference implementations for local development isolation, with no evidence of credential exfiltration or prompt injection. The skill should publish with warnings because copied scripts can write env files, auto-load credentials, execute local commands, and remove Docker resources.

medium_findings.title: Generated Env Files May Contain Credentials
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally generates and auto-loads .localnet.env data, including example database URLs and project-specific base env merging. This is legitimate for local development, but copied implementations may place credentials in generated files that are sourced by direnv or shell scripts. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced sections explicitly generate, merge, write, and source env files. The behavior is documented and local, so risk depends on project secrets rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Local Command Execution and Docker Cleanup Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill includes shell and Docker commands for listing, starting, and cleaning local instances. These commands are expected for this devops workflow, but cleanup examples can remove containers and volumes if copied without reviewing scope. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Docker removal commands and shell cleanup are directly present. They target localnet-prefixed resources and include warnings, which lowers confidence of abuse but not operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Reference Code Executes Local Git and Port Probing Commands
medium_findings.description: Advanced examples use execSync for hardcoded git commands and shell fallbacks probe local ports. The commands support automatic naming and allocation, but any generated script should keep arguments fixed or sanitized. Verdict: NEEDS_REVIEW.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution patterns are visible and mostly hardcoded or sanitized. Legitimate use is likely, but generated variants could introduce injection if they pass unsanitized input to shells.

low_findings.title: Localhost Network Indicators Are Benign Examples
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs and the hardcoded 0.0.0.0 listener are used to demonstrate localhost routing, browser isolation, and port availability checks. No evidence found of outbound network exfiltration. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited endpoints are localhost or local Docker/Tilt examples. I found no external collection endpoint or secret transmission.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner False Positives for SAM and Weak Crypto
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM and weak cryptography alerts do not match the cited context. The flagged lines are descriptive text or unrelated examples, not credential database access or cryptographic operations. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the exact cited lines shows browser storage text, the skill description, and CI naming guidance. There is no SAM database path or hash algorithm use there.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment File Generation and Auto-Loading
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference implementation writes .localnet.env and recommends dotenv_if_exists. Treat generated env files as sensitive and keep them out of version control. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The implementation writes and loads env files and also recommends gitignore entries. This is intentional but security-relevant.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bulk Docker Resource Removal
dangerous_patterns.description: Cleanup examples remove containers, volumes, and networks matching localnet prefixes. The pattern is useful but destructive if naming scopes are too broad. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly remove Docker resources. They are scoped by prefix and warned as dangerous, so this is operational risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Parallel local project instances often collide on ports, Docker resources, and browser state. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code create gen-env workflows that isolate each instance.
actual_capabilities: Designs gen-env commands that allocate separate local ports for each workspace.
Creates naming patterns for Docker Compose projects, networks, volumes, and containers.
Uses name.localhost hosts to separate browser cookies and local storage.
Provides TypeScript and shell reference implementations for generated env files.
Reviews existing gen-env scripts against isolation, cleanup, and lockfile checklists.
Shows integration patterns for direnv, Docker Compose, Tilt, and CI workflows.
limitations: Does not run or validate generated scripts unless the assistant executes project commands separately.
Reference code must be adapted to each project service list and env file layout.
Generated env files may contain sensitive values and need local gitignore controls.
Docker cleanup examples can remove local resources if names are scoped incorrectly.

use_cases.title: Run Parallel Feature Branches
use_cases.description: Create separate ports, hosts, and Docker resources for multiple worktrees on one machine.

use_cases.title: Standardize Local Dev Scripts
use_cases.description: Build a repeatable gen-env command that teams can use across Docker Compose and direnv setups.

use_cases.title: Audit Environment Isolation
use_cases.description: Review an existing local environment script for port conflicts, shared volumes, browser state collisions, and unsafe cleanup.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic gen-env Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a gen-env command for this project. It should accept a workspace name, allocate ports, write .localnet.env, and avoid shared Docker resources.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Current Setup
prompt_templates.scenario: Check an existing local environment script
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my gen-env or local setup files. Identify port collisions, shared browser state, unsafe cleanup, and missing lockfile behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Add Docker Compose Isolation
prompt_templates.prompt: Update my Docker Compose workflow so each workspace gets unique project names, networks, volumes, container prefixes, and generated service URLs.

prompt_templates.title: Design CI-Safe Environment Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a CI-safe gen-env workflow with unique job names, random local ports, isolated env files, deterministic cleanup, and clear failure handling.

output_examples.input: Create a gen-env workflow for a web app with API, Postgres, Redis, and Storybook.
output_examples.output: A proposed command design with workspace naming, allocated port keys, generated hostnames, Docker Compose variables, and cleanup behavior.

output_examples.input: Review my local development scripts for isolation problems.
output: Findings about shared ports and volumes.
Recommended changes for COMPOSE_PROJECT_NAME and instance hosts.
Warnings about generated env files and Docker cleanup scope.

output_examples.input: Adapt this pattern for CI jobs that run in parallel.
output_examples.output: A CI-oriented plan using unique run identifiers, fresh port allocation, generated env loading, and always-run cleanup steps.

best_practices: Keep generated env and lock files out of version control unless they contain only safe placeholders.
Require explicit workspace names and validate them before deriving hosts or Docker resource names.
Scope cleanup commands to the exact Compose project or instance prefix before removing volumes.
anti_patterns: Using only different localhost ports while sharing cookies, local storage, or auth state.
Running broad Docker cleanup commands without checking the target project prefix.
Copying base env secrets into generated files without gitignore and review controls.

faq.question: What does this skill generate?
faq.answer: It helps create or review gen-env commands that isolate local development instances by name, ports, hosts, Docker resources, and env files.

faq.question: Does it require Docker?
faq.answer: Docker is not required for every idea, but many examples target Docker Compose projects with local services.

faq.question: Why use name.localhost instead of localhost?
faq.answer: Different localhost ports can still share browser state. Separate subdomains help isolate cookies and local storage.

faq.question: Can it work with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is documented for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows that edit project scripts and configuration.

faq.question: Is the generated .localnet.env safe to commit?
faq.answer: Usually no. Treat generated env files as local artifacts because they can contain service URLs, ports, and copied secret values.

faq.question: What should reviewers check first?
faq.answer: Check workspace name validation, env file handling, Docker cleanup scope, and whether commands use sanitized inputs.
